name: Dr. Ramesh Patel, Clinical Research Coordinator
summary: Ramesh runs 2-3 concurrent clinical trials at a mid-size academic medical center. He spends his days shepherding case report forms through IRB workflows and preparing for monitor visits, balancing patient safety against aggressive enrollment deadlines.
goals: Hit enrollment targets for each active trial without protocol deviations
Pass every monitor visit with zero major findings
pains: CRF queries pile up faster than they can be resolved
IRB amendments slow down protocol changes by weeks
motivations: Publishable results that advance the field
objections: New tools must be 21 CFR Part 11 compliant or they cannot be used
sample_quotes: I cannot enroll the next cohort until the IRB signs off on amendment three.
The monitor flagged seventeen open queries on the CRF last Tuesday.

journey_stages.mindset: Looking for anything that reduces CRF query turnaround
content_preferences: Case studies from other academic sites

source_evidence.claim: IRB amendment friction is a primary pain

name: Sarah Kim, Small-Business Tax Accountant
summary: Sarah is a partner at a four-person boutique tax firm serving small businesses and S-corps. From January through April she barely sleeps, juggling Drake, QuickBooks Online, and a wall of client 1099s and K-1s.
goals: Close out tax season without a single late return
Grow the S-corp advisory book by 20% year over year
pains: Clients send 1099s in every imaginable format, most of them wrong
EBITDA add-back calculations have to be redone whenever a client sends an amended P&L
motivations: Trusted-advisor relationships with long-term small-business clients
Reliable recurring revenue outside of tax season
objections: Cannot take on a tool that does not integrate cleanly with Drake
channels: AICPA events and local CPA society meetings
decision_triggers: A botched client handoff from the previous preparer
sample_quotes: If the client wants to take the S-corp election, we have until March 15th to file the 2553.
Their EBITDA add-back wipes out half the owner's comp and we still need another week of books.

journey_stages.mindset: Frantic in March, receptive only in May
Wants a 30-day pilot, no annual contract
key_actions: Runs a parallel test on three returns

source_evidence.claim: Drake integration is a hard requirement

name: Ms. Linda Thompson, Public Elementary School Teacher
summary: Linda teaches 3rd grade in a Title I district. She writes lesson plans aligned to state standards on Sunday nights, runs DIBELS assessments monthly, and knows every one of her 24 students' IEP accommodations by heart.
goals: Get every student to grade-level reading by spring benchmarks
Differentiate lessons so IEP students hit their accommodations without falling behind
pains: Sunday night lesson plan writing eats the entire weekend
DIBELS data lives in a separate portal from the gradebook
motivations: Seeing a struggling reader break through
Being trusted by families in the community
objections: The district will not approve a tool that stores student data outside approved vendors
channels: Teachers Pay Teachers and district PD days
decision_triggers: A new state-standards update or curriculum adoption
sample_quotes: I need a lesson plan template that maps directly to the state ELA standards.
Marcus's IEP says extended time on assessments, so his DIBELS goal is adjusted.

journey_stages.mindset: Always looking for a time-saver that respects student privacy
Needs buy-in from the principal and tech coordinator

name: Marco Russo, Independent Restaurant Owner
summary: Marco owns a 40-seat neighborhood Italian spot. He runs the line most nights, reconciles POS tapes at 1am, and obsesses over food cost variance because a two-point swing is the difference between making rent and not.
goals: Keep food cost variance under 32% every week
Fill the 6pm–8pm window on Tuesdays and Wednesdays
pains: Toast POS data does not roll cleanly into QuickBooks, so every month is manual
Mise en place takes an extra thirty minutes when a prep cook calls out
motivations: Pride in a regular table that has been coming for ten years
Keeping the staff paid through slow months
objections: No subscription above fifty dollars a month unless it pays for itself in a week
channels: Restaurant-owner Facebook groups and local supplier reps
decision_triggers: A bad month of food cost that he cannot explain
sample_quotes: My food cost variance jumped four points last week and I still do not know why.
If the POS and the books do not talk, I am doing it twice at one in the morning.

journey_stages.mindset: Skeptical of anything that sounds like 'restaurant tech'
content_preferences: Other independents saying it actually worked

journey_stages.mindset: Will give it a week before pulling the plug
key_actions: Pilots it during a slow midweek

name: Elena Vasquez, Senior Urban Planner
summary: Elena is a senior planner in a municipal planning department. She prepares zoning variance packets for the board, steers the comp plan update through public hearings, and navigates CEQA review timelines that outlast most elected officials.
goals: Get the comp plan update adopted before the election cycle turns over
Move routine variance packets through the board in a single cycle
pains: CEQA review adds months to every project and the timelines are non-negotiable
Public hearing comments arrive in PDFs, emails, and handwritten cards — none of it searchable
motivations: Shaping a city she will retire in
A council that actually reads the staff report
objections: Procurement rules block any tool the city has not already bought
channels: APA conferences and the state planning association listserv
decision_triggers: A council directive tied to the next comp plan cycle
sample_quotes: The variance packet has to be in front of the board two weeks before the hearing.
We cannot advance the staff report until CEQA review closes out.

key_actions: Chats with peers at other cities

journey_stages.mindset: Needs a master services agreement on file
key_actions: Loops in city IT and legal
